schools.summary: A polished literary preface (Robertson, Ellicott); 'the many' are earlier narratives — perhaps including Matthew and Mark, perhaps fragments — with no disparagement.
The fathers contrast Luke's inspired certainty with the presumptuous uninspired Gospels (Eusebius, Ambrose, Bede).
Calvin: Luke claims certainty without accusing predecessors of imposture, counteracting harmful 'historical sketches'; Henry: moved by the Spirit yet as a reasonable creature.

debates.question: Who are 'the many' who had written before Luke?

sides.position: Earlier fragments/narratives, possibly including Matthew and Mark
Not the canonical Gospels (Matthew, Mark, John)

themes: forasmuch as many have taken in hand

schools.summary: Luke candidly relies on the dependable apostolic tradition (oral and written), disclaiming being himself an eyewitness; 'delivered' the technical term of transmission (Ellicott, Robertson).
Cyril/Origen: 'eyewitnesses of the word' agrees with John 1:14 — the Word made visible in the flesh, more excellent than the voice.

themes: eyewitnesses and ministers of the word

schools.summary: Luke 'traced all things from their source' (step by step, Ellicott, Calvin); his inspiration did not set aside but presided over his diligent investigation, keeping him from error (Barnes, Clarke).
Calvin: the verb denotes close, laborious investigation, treading in the footsteps so that nothing escapes.

Luke's aim: that Theophilus (already catechized in the gospel facts) might know their 'certainty' — an impregnable historical foundation (Robertson); the believer should press from elementary to settled, well-grounded assurance (Clarke, Henry).

themes: that thou mightest know the certainty

schools.summary: Herod the foreign king's reign signals Messiah's coming (the sceptre departed from Judah, Genesis 49:10); John of priestly descent heralds a change of priesthood (Bede); the old conceive before the virgin (Chrysostom).
Henry: the houses of Aaron and David honoured in their latter days (John and Christ); the foreign king marks Shiloh's time.
Zacharias of the course of Abia (the eighth of 24), not the high priest (Lightfoot); a righteous priestly couple in Herod's days.
Ambrose: 'before God' is the perfect praise — approved by Him who cannot be deceived, not a flattering reputation among men; commandment and justification distinguished.
Henry: truly righteous (not sinless), shown by works not talk, devotion and conduct agreeing, universal in aim, blameless of open scandal — ministers especially to be examples.
'Blameless' guarded against perfectionism — sincere and real righteousness, free from open scandal, the constant aim though not flawless.
The barrenness no mark of sin but ordained, that seeing the barren conceive we might believe the greater wonder of the virgin's birth (Chrysostom, Theophylact).
Henry: the long-barren, like the mothers of Isaac, Samson, Samuel, bear eminent sons — God keeps his people waiting and doubles the blessing's worth when it comes.

Zacharias serves in his appointed course (each of the 24 divisions twice yearly) 'before God' — the solemn, ordained old-covenant worship into which the extraordinary breaks; God meets his servant in the path of duty.
The burning of incense, assigned by lot, was a rare high honour (once in a lifetime); the incense the appointed symbol of prayer ascending (Psalm 141:2), accompanying the people's prayers.
Calvin: Zacharias prayed as he entered in the people's name; the lot ordering this moment was God's providence appointing the great announcement.

themes: his lot was to burn incense

schools.summary: The multitude prayed without while the priest offered incense within — the prayers ascending with the smoke (Revelation 8:3-4), embodying old-covenant mediated worship; the public, solemn setting of the announcement.
The angel appears at the golden altar of incense, on the right (south, honour) side, suddenly to Zacharias ministering alone within at the hour of prayer.
The fathers read the right side as the side of favour and good tidings; the angel at the altar of intercession declares that prayer is heard (Ambrose, Bede, Origen).

themes: an angel of the Lord appeared
on the right side of the altar

schools.summary: Zacharias's terror is the natural reaction of mortal flesh to a sudden heavenly apparition, especially in the holy place.
Calvin: God's glory humbles the saints (not swallowing them in despair as the reprobate) to behold him with reverence — then he raises them up; holy fear the right approach to God.
'Thy prayer is heard' — for a son, or (Ellicott) for the kingdom, receiving more than he asked; 'John' = 'Jehovah is gracious,' a pledge of grace (Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: his long-poured prayer at length heard; the name commends the grace his mission brings to all, weighty for being given before birth; Wesley: an answer of peace when the case seemed most helpless.

themes: thou shalt call his name John

schools.summary: Joy and 'extreme exultation' (Robertson) at John's birth, fruitful of the joy of many (Ellicott); a child of prayer a source of comfort, turning multitudes from sin (Clarke).
Calvin: the joy public not merely domestic — John given as prophet to the whole people, who rejoice that a prophet leading to salvation is born (rebuking the carnal feast).

themes: many shall rejoice at his birth

schools.summary: John a lifelong Nazarite (Numbers 6:4, like Samson/Samuel), abstaining from wine; the lower stimulation's absence implies capacity for the higher (Ephesians 5:18, Ellicott); filled with the Spirit from the womb (a Hebraism).
Ambrose/Bede: his greatness is of soul, not body; the Nazarite freed from wine is filled with the Spirit's grace; he bore witness to the Spirit before birth.
Calvin: the abstinence a visible token of consecration, not the substance of holiness; 'filled' = uncommon abundance of the Spirit's gifts, a token of future rank.

themes: great in the sight of the Lord

schools.summary: John turns many of Israel to God by repentance, fulfilling the Elijah promise (Malachi 4:6); not all, but many were converted under his ministry (Gill, Barnes).

Calvin: conversion is God's own work (surpassing creation) wrought through the minister as his hand; the outward doctrine efficacious only joined to the Spirit, turning those God effectually calls (Romans 10:17).

John goes before 'him' (the Lord their God = Christ, a proof of his deity, Barnes) in the spirit and power of Elijah (the antitype, not in person, John 1:21; Mark 9:12), reconciling families and preparing a people.

Calvin: no Pythagorean transmigration but the same Spirit's power as in Elijah; he restores from discord to reconciliation grounded in faith ('the wisdom of the just') — accursed the unity apart from God.

themes: in the spirit and power of Elias
turn the hearts of the fathers

schools.summary: Zacharias doubts and demands a sign, the angel's testimony should have sufficed (Barnes); contrast with Mary: he disbelieved the fact, she asked only the manner (Wesley).

Calvin: judged by the heart's state, his doubt was culpable (ascribing too little to God's power, questioning whether God deserved credit), yet partial — he held the root of faith but wavered on one point.
Chrysostom: disputes of this kind mark a rebellious spirit — God's declaration should be received in faith.
Gabriel ('strength of God'), who announced Daniel's seventy weeks (Daniel 9:21), names himself — his standing in God's presence and divine commission rebuking the doubt and confirming the message.
Bede/Chrysostom: when an angel promises it is not right to doubt; the messenger relates nothing of his own but the message of him who sends him.

themes: stand in the presence of God

schools.summary: The dumbness both the demanded sign and the chastisement of unbelief, proportioned to the fault (the doubting tongue silenced), lasting until the birth confirms the word.
Chrysostom/Theophylact: the priest more smitten for doubting where he should have been an example; tempered with mercy, removed when the event proved the word.
The people wait for the priest's blessing (Numbers 6:23) and marvel at his delay — caused by the vision and his dumbness; their wonder prepares them to perceive he had seen a vision (v.22).
Greek Exposition: amid the people's surprise and suspicions, Zacharias 'told by his silence what he secretly endured.'
Zacharias comes out speechless, beckoning; the people rightly infer he had seen a vision — his dumbness (the chastisement, v.20) becoming the public sign attesting the visitation.
Theophylact/Ambrose: he signifies by nodding what he cannot speak — a nod declaring the will without expressing it.
Zacharias, though dumb, faithfully completes his week's ministration before returning home — perseverance in duty under chastisement; the return prepares for Elizabeth's conception (v.24).

themes: the days of his ministration accomplished
he departed to his own house

schools.summary: Elizabeth conceives per the promise and hides five months — from modest reverence, grateful devotion, or to let the condition become certain; her seclusion serving to confirm the sign to Mary (vv.36, 39).

Barrenness was a reproach among the Jews (cf. Rachel, Genesis 30:23); Elizabeth gratefully ascribes the conception wholly to God who 'looked on her,' modelling the right response to answered prayer (like Hannah).

themes: the Lord hath dealt with me

schools.summary: In the sixth month Gabriel ('strength of God,' bearer of the highest tidings) is sent to a virgin — answering the serpent sent to Eve (Bede); the obscure Nazareth marks the humility of the work (Gloss).
The timing links the two annunciations; Gabriel sent to lowly Nazareth in Galilee, a remote town in no reputation (Henry).
Henry: the same Gabriel carries on the one work of redemption; the lowly place displays its humility.

themes: the angel Gabriel sent from God

schools.summary: The espousal was providential — shielding Mary's reputation, giving a witness (Joseph) to her purity, fulfilling the law, and baffling the devil who could cast no suspicion on an espoused woman's offspring (Ambrose, Origen).
Mary a virgin of David's line (as the throne-promise of v.32 requires), espoused to Joseph of David's house (Bede, Robertson); her virginity necessary for the miraculous sinless birth (Henry).

'Highly favoured' (kecharitomene) = endowed with grace (Ephesians 1:6), one who has received grace, not a fountain of it; the Vulgate's 'full of grace' misleads (Ellicott, Robertson); 'blessed among women' omitted in the best MSS here.
Calvin: the word denotes God's undeserved favour — Mary's blessing flows from the same source as ours; the Papists wrongly turned the salutation into a prayer (so Wesley: no warrant for adoration).
The salutation announces grace and joy reversing Eve's sorrow (Gregory of Nyssa); 'the Lord is with thee' — in her heart and womb (Augustine).

debates.question: Does 'highly favoured' mean 'full of grace' (a fountain of grace) warranting Marian devotion?

sides.position: No — it means one endowed with received grace, from God's undeserved favour; no warrant for adoration
Mary received the fullness of grace wholly infused (the fathers' devotional reading)

themes: Hail, thou that art highly favoured
highly favoured vs full of grace

schools.summary: Mary troubled and puzzled (the same word as for Zacharias), yet composed, pondering 'what manner of salutation' — troubled at the saying (the high honour ascribed her), not merely the vision.
Calvin: her trouble was reverent awe at heavenly glory, not unbelief; her pondering shows an attentive, composed mind — neither Eve's inconstancy nor Zacharias's insensibility (Greek Exposition).

themes: she was troubled at his saying

schools.summary: 'Fear not... thou hast found favour' (grace, the word of v.28) — eminent mercy in being chosen mother of the Messiah (Barnes); 'favour' to avoid technical associations (Ellicott).
Calvin: favour is found not by seeking but by its being freely offered — undeserved grace, the gracious choice of God; God gives grace to the humble (Chrysostom).

themes: thou hast found favour with God

schools.summary: The conception foretold (Isaiah 7:14, Immanuel); the name Jesus = Saviour, given by the angel, containing the promise of salvation ('he shall save his people'); what perplexed Mary was the birth before her marriage (Ellicott).
Calvin: 'bring forth' establishes Christ's true humanity against Marcion/Manichaeus (not a phantom but real fruit of her womb); the name Joshua bore in shadow now fulfilled in substance.
Drawn from Isaiah 9:6-7 and 2 Samuel 7:14; the throne of David fulfilled not in a revived earthly kingdom but in the spiritual, everlasting kingdom (Ellicott, Basil, Cyril).

Calvin: 'Son of the Highest' is Christ's peculiar prerogative as the only-begotten (Hebrews 1:5; Psalm 2:7), distinct from John's greatness and our adoptive sonship; the one Person in two natures, against Nestorius (Bede).

themes: the throne of his father David

schools.summary: 'The house of Jacob' = the spiritual Israel of Jews and Gentiles joined by faith (Gill, Barnes); the everlasting kingdom (Daniel 7:14); the mediatorial kingdom perfected, not abolished (Ellicott, 1 Corinthians 15:24).
Calvin: Christ's throne fixed in Israel to subdue the world — all joined by faith to Abraham are the true Israel; God preserves a remnant (Romans 11:5); true perpetuity in the glory to come.

themes: reign over the house of Jacob

schools.summary: Ambrose/Gregory of Nyssa: Mary believes the fact (the virgin birth foretold) and asks only the manner — resting on her chastity, deeming her inviolability precious; unlike Zacharias's doubt.

Calvin: a question of faith (not lowering God's power); sharply rejects the 'vow of perpetual virginity' reading as absurd and unfounded — no monastic life existed among the Jews; her words mean only her present untouched state.
Mary reverently seeks the manner, not a sign (Ellicott); she knew the Messiah was to be virgin-born, so asks how, not whether (Gill).

themes: seeing I know not a man

schools.summary: The 'overshadow' = the Shekinah cloud of God's presence (Exodus 40:38, Robertson); the Spirit's quickening work (Genesis 1:2); the body created directly by God, pure and free from the fall's corruption (Barnes).

Calvin: the conception sinless (the Spirit kept him pure, 1 Peter 1:19); the eternal Son, who was Son from the beginning, appears also as Son in the flesh — the name applied to both natures (against Servetus).
Clarke: the title 'Son of God' rests on the supernatural birth — he denies the 'eternal Sonship' as self-contradictory; Wesley affirms both eternal deity and the title on this account.

debates.question: Does the title 'Son of God' here rest on the supernatural birth or on eternal generation?

sides.position: On the supernatural birth — 'eternal Son' is self-contradictory
On his eternal Sonship, now manifested in the flesh

themes: the Holy Ghost shall come upon thee
the power of the Highest overshadow thee
that holy thing the Son of God
the virgin conception and two natures

schools.summary: A confirming sign — Elizabeth's conception in old age, six months along — to strengthen Mary's faith for the greater miracle (Bede); the barren conceiving makes way for belief in the virgin birth (Lightfoot).

Gregory of Nazianzus: the kinship of Mary (David) and Elizabeth (Aaron) unites the royal and priestly lines, fitting Christ who is King and Priest; the angel descends to sensible things to persuade her (Chrysostom).
Calvin: God graciously supports weak faith with confirming signs, the more indulgently as our faith is weaker.
'With God nothing shall be impossible' — the same word spoken to Sarah (Genesis 18:14) confirming the virgin conception; 'no word' (rhema) of God shall fail (Robertson, Clarke).
Calvin: God's power must not be imagined beyond his word; true faith apprehends his power 'not in the air but with its results' — confidence joined to the certainty of his promise.

themes: with God nothing shall be impossible
is anything too hard for the Lord

schools.summary: Calvin: Mary's surrender is the real proof of faith — she holds her mind captive, not disputing; faith makes us obedient servants, unbelief rebels; the climax of the annunciation.
Ambrose: her humility and devotion — chosen as mother, she calls herself handmaid; 'be it unto me' the readiness of duty and the conception of the wish.
Resignation to God's will and faith in the promise (Barnes); half-conscious of coming suffering as well as blessedness, she anticipates 'Not my will but Thine' (Ellicott).

themes: behold the handmaid of the Lord
be it unto me according to thy word

schools.summary: Ambrose: Mary, filled with God, goes with haste — the grace of the Spirit knows no slow workings; her humility (the younger to the elder, the higher to the lower) a pattern of charity; Origen: Christ hastening to sanctify John in the womb.
Mary journeys promptly to the hill country of Judah (where Zacharias dwelt) to visit Elizabeth — the sign the angel gave (v.36), to confirm her faith and for mutual comfort (Henry).

themes: Mary arose and went with haste
humility and the eagerness of grace

schools.summary: Mary enters Zacharias's house and greets Elizabeth — a simple act displaying her humility (the chosen mother greeting first her elder kinswoman) and setting in motion the wonder of the visitation (vv.41-42).
Ambrose: she was first to salute, the younger to the elder — the humility of the chosen mother; her greeting the occasion of John's leaping witness (Greek Exposition).
The leaping a common thing (Genesis 25:22), but Elisabeth filled with the Spirit to understand Mary's news; the account ultimately from the Virgin herself (Ellicott).
Clarke: the fulfilment of the promise that John would be filled with the Spirit from the womb (v.15).
Calvin: the extraordinary thing lay chiefly in the mother, suddenly endued with the Spirit, not in conscious piety of the embryo.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on Pentecost, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The loud cry of ecstatic excitement; 'blessed' a Hebraistic superlative; Elisabeth repeats the angel's greeting (v.28); contrast with Jael (Judges 5:24).
Calvin: not Mary and Christ on an equal footing — she is blessed for the sake of her child, her deeper blessedness being her spiritual renewal, not the bearing of Christ in the womb.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on Philip in Samaria, tangential to the verse.

themes: blessed the fruit of the womb

schools.summary: 'The mother of my Lord' uses 'Lord' in its highest sense (Psalm 110:1); only the Spirit could reveal Mary's role; the prophetic insight.
Calvin: the happy medium — valuing God's gifts in Mary without envy or superstition.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): the Spirit wrought in Elisabeth, who calls Mary the mother of her Lord.
Gill: Elisabeth names the babe's extraordinary leaping as the signal by which she knew Mary was the mother of her Lord.
The first NT beatitude (cf. John 20:29); 'hoti' may be 'that' or 'because', the two renderings weighing whether the fulfilment hung on her faith.
Gill and Calvin contrast Mary's ready faith with Zacharias's unbelief — a virgin believed the incredible without a sign where the priest doubted a lesser thing.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on God forming man in the womb, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: Ambrose: as evil came by a woman, so good is introduced by women, Mary uttering the fuller prophecy; to magnify is to ascribe greatness, not make God great.
'Magnify' = extol (Psalm 34:3); a counterpart of Hannah's song (1 Samuel 2:1-10); a few Latin MSS assign it to Elisabeth, but Mary is correct; its place in worship since Caesarius of Arles.
Basil: the Virgin with lofty thoughts contemplates the boundless mystery, magnifying God.
Calvin: the song's three parts — thanksgiving, celebration of God's power, application to redemption; a warning against praising God without heart.

debates.question: Is the Magnificat Mary's or Elisabeth's?

sides.position: A few Latin MSS assign it to Elisabeth

schools.summary: Basil: the first-fruit of the Spirit is joy; the Virgin exults with an unspeakable bounding of heart.
Ambrose: let every one have the spirit of Mary to rejoice in the Lord; by faith Christ is the fruit of all.
The aorist 'rejoiced' (exult); soul and spirit near synonyms; God called 'Saviour' as he saves from sin (Habakkuk 3:18).
Wesley: she rejoiced in salvation by faith, a blessing common to all believers, more than in being his mother after the flesh.

themes: joy the pattern for all believers

schools.summary: Augustine: Mary's lowliness the heavenly ladder by which God descended; the Greek Expositor: content with things lowly, now chosen to unspeakable counsels.

'Low estate' (tapeinosis) a mean condition — the carpenter's bride to be Messiah's mother; God no respecter of persons (Romans 2:11; Psalm 138:6); 'all generations' is not Mariolatry (Robertson, Clarke).
Calvin: tapeinosis denotes a despicable condition, not modesty; she disclaims all merit, ascribing all to God's undeserved goodness.

themes: all generations shall call me blessed

schools.summary: Augustine: a creature gave birth to the Creator; Titus of Bostra: the great thing is the virgin conception overcoming nature, the miracle thrown back on the Worker's power; Bede ties it to 'magnify'.
The 'great things' distinguished mercies; 'holy is his name' = freedom from sin (Psalm 111:9); Clarke renders the root 'abundant goodness'.
Calvin: the second part of the song, turning from her own case to God's power and holiness in general.
Bede: Mary turns from special gifts to God's general dealings — in every nation he that fears God is accepted; the mercy extends to eternity.
The fear is reverential and filial, not dread (Acts 10:2); the words foreshadow the gospel to the Gentiles, who fear God in every nation (Acts 10:35).
Gill: the fear describes, though it does not cause, those on whom mercy is shown — the vessels of mercy.
Bede and Theophylact: the 'arm' is the incarnate Son, God's Word by whom he made the world, vanquishing nature in the virgin birth and scattering the proud.
God's finger, hand, and arm mark ascending power (Exodus 15:16); six aorists; close parallel to Hannah (1 Samuel 2:3).
Wesley: Mary speaks prophetically of what God was about to do by the Messiah.

themes: the arm as the incarnate Son

schools.summary: Bede: through all generations the proud fall and the humble are exalted; Cyril (in Bede): the mighty in knowledge — evil spirits, the wise Gentiles, the Scribes — put down.
'The mighty' (dynastas, whence 'dynasty') rulers cast from thrones (1 Timothy 6:15); 'low degree' echoes Mary's 'low estate' (v.48); a reference to David over Saul.
Calvin ascribes such changes to the providence and judgment of God, raising the obscure in the room of the fallen.
The Gloss: the verse passes to the impoverishing of the rich and filling of the poor.
Basil: all mankind hungered save the Jews, who had the oracles, yet not resting on the Incarnate Word were sent empty.
The poor come for bread, the rich with lofty hearts sent empty; the verse anticipates the beatitudes (Luke 6:21), literal and spiritual.
The Gloss: he helped Israel ('him who sees God') by becoming visible; Basil: the spiritual Israel seeing God by faith; Theophylact allows Israel after the flesh too.
'Holpen' = lay hold to succour; 'servant' (paidos) not 'son'; recalls the servant of Jehovah (Isaiah 41:8); the reviving of David's fallen house.
Calvin: God lays hold of fallen Israel by the hand to lift him, the nation's restoration beyond ordinary hope.
'Abraham and his seed' belongs to 'remember his mercy... for ever' (v.54), not in apposition to 'fathers'; the mercy to Abraham working still (Genesis 22:17-18).
Gill: the fathers are David, Jacob, Isaac, Abraham; Wesley: 'his seed' the spiritual seed, all true believers.
'Three months' the rabbinic span for ascertaining pregnancy, confirming the sign of Elisabeth's conception (v.36); debate whether she stayed for John's birth.
Ambrose: she abode for the increase of so great a prophet, her presence conferring grace; Theophylact reckons the completed nine months.
Calvin: John's birth was distinguished by miracles promising something great; Mary stayed probably till the birth.
Ambrose: 'fullness' used only at the birth of the righteous, whose life is full; Chrysostom: the Lord delayed her delivery to increase her joy and fame.
Henry: conceived by miracle, John kept the ordinary course of nature; promised mercies come when the full time is come.
'Cousins' better 'kindred'; 'shewed great mercy' literally 'magnified his mercy', an echo of the Magnificat (v.46); the imperfect of continual mutual joy.
Clarke: to rejoice with the favoured is a duty of humanity, charity, and religion; the reproach of barrenness now rolled away.
Chrysostom: circumcision, a sign given to Abraham, ceased through Christ, baptism in its place, though John was first rightly circumcised.
Bede: naming him after his father = the Jews preferring the Law's righteousness over the grace of faith (the name John = 'grace of God'); Ambrose: Elisabeth named him by prophecy.
The name given at circumcision (Genesis 21:3-4), often the father's; the conative imperfect 'they tried to call' him Zacharias.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on Antioch, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The name the angel appointed (v.13), Zacharias having told her by writing; John = 'the grace or mercy of Jehovah' (Clarke).
Calvin and Wesley: she spoke by revelation or impulse, though Zacharias may have informed her of the angel's command in writing.
The Jewish custom of naming children after ancestors, from reverence and to keep genealogies clear; John a common name but not in this family (Gill).
Making signs shows Zacharias was deaf as well as dumb (otherwise they would have asked); the imperfect 'made signs' and indirect question; rabbinic notice of the deaf-mute communicating by nods.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the triumphal entry, not this verse.

schools.summary: The 'writing table' (pinakidion) a wax-coated wooden board written with a stylus; the emphatic present 'His name is John' — already fixed, not for debate; the general marvel confirms his deafness.
'Immediately' (a Lukan word) and the zeugma — 'opened' suits the mouth, 'loosed' the tongue; nine months dumb, speech restored at the naming, used first to praise God.
Calvin: God honoured the prophet's birth by restoring his father's speech, delayed till that day to fix eyes on John; Zacharias praised God, uniting his dishonour with God's glory.
Not terror but religious reverence at the supernatural (Acts 9:31); the events 'noised abroad' (imperfect) through the hill country; the spread from neighbourhood to district.
Theophylact: they marvelled at the silence and the speech, that all might believe something great in the child; signs prepare the way for the forerunner.
Calvin: the fear came from a feeling of the divine power, which God's works ought to stir in us.
'What then' (not 'who') predicts a marvellous career; they laid it up as Mary did (Luke 2:19); 'the hand of the Lord' = God's guidance and favour (Acts 11:21), a Hebrew idiom.
Wesley: 'the hand of the Lord' the peculiar power and blessing of God.

outliers.note: A passage on the sevenfold Spirit, only loosely related to the verse.

schools.summary: Ambrose: God restores beyond expectation — the long-silent prophesies; let none despair, for God knows how to change his sentence; Chrysostom: he received the Spirit fully.
'Prophesied' here an inspired utterance of praise (1 Corinthians 14:24-25) and foretelling; nearly every phrase from Psalms and Prophets, the highest Messianic meaning.
Henry: God not only forgave his unbelief but filled him with the Spirit, his once-silenced mouth made the vehicle of prophecy.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the Spirit at Pentecost, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: 'Hath visited' = looked upon to help (Matthew 25:43), the four silent centuries ended; 'redemption' originally political with a spiritual basis (vv.75, 77); the hymn in Western worship since Caesarius of Arles.
Clarke: two benefits — visited and ransomed; God visits his people in the Incarnation, spoken prophetically as already done.
Calvin: thanksgiving for the fulfilment of the redemption promised in Christ; 'God of Israel' because the Redeemer was promised to Abraham's seed.

themes: 'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel'

schools.summary: Theophylact: God risen up to tread down the evil spirits; Chrysostom: the horn means power, glory, honour, from horned beasts.
The horn a symbol of strength and so a mighty Saviour able to save (Psalm 132:17), 'in the house of David' since Christ came of David's line.
Bede: Christ's kingdom the horn of salvation, reaching beyond the world; David and Solomon consecrated with the horn of oil.
Calvin: saving power; when David's throne was cast down, God made the horn to bud (Psalm 132:17).
Theophylact cites Micah 5:2; the Greek Expositor: God spoke through them, not of man; Bede: all the OT a constant prophecy of Christ from Adam onward.
All the prophets foretold the Messiah — Jacob (Genesis 49:10), Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15), Isaiah; 'since the world began' = 'from of old'; the first gospel (Genesis 3:15).
Calvin: all the prophets adduced as witnesses, that the salvation may not be thought doubtful for its novelty.

themes: by the mouth of his prophets

schools.summary: Origen (in Bede): not bodily but ghostly enemies; the Lord came mighty in battle to destroy them.
Clarke: spoken by the Spirit, the salvation is spiritual — Satan, death, sin (Genesis 3:15); Ellicott allows Roman and Herodian foes in Zacharias's mind.
Calvin: the church's chief war is with Satan, not flesh and blood, though Christ delivers from outward foes too.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the resurrection (Jonah), tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: Bede: the covenant with Abraham, to whom the gathering of the Gentiles was promised; Theophylact: mercy to the forefathers fulfilling all their hopes; Basil: God's word stands in place of an oath.
Ellicott: an echo of the names — John ('the Lord be merciful'), Zacharias ('whom Jehovah remembers'), Elisabeth ('the oath of my God'); mercy to children is kindness to fathers.
Calvin: the gracious mercy of God the fountain of redemption, God mindful of his promise.
The oath of Genesis 22:16-18, God swearing by himself (Hebrews 6:13-14) to bless Abraham's seed, now fulfilled; the oppression of the Gentiles in Zacharias's mind.
Calvin: the oath expresses the firmness of God's truth, condescending to use his name for the support of our weakness.
The end to which the covenant pointed — service of worship and obedience 'without fear' of enemies or death; 'serve' (latreuein) the service rendered to God.
Chrysostom: Christ unites two things not easily found together — deliverance from danger and a glorious, fearless life.
Calvin: the redeemed are to consecrate themselves wholly to God, the final cause of redemption being a godly life that glorifies him (1 Thessalonians 4:7).

outliers.note: The note here is 'Have faith in God', not bearing on the verse.

schools.summary: 'Holiness' (hosiotes) the Godward and 'righteousness' (dikaiosyne) the manward aspect of conduct (Ephesians 4:24); 'before him', inward and spiritual, all the days to death.
Chrysostom: holiness our duty toward God, righteousness toward man, done before God not men.
Calvin: the two tables of the law — holiness the first, righteousness the second — frame the proper service of God.
John 'prophet of the Highest' and forerunner, going before the Lord — an echo of Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3 — the 'Lord' being Jehovah/the Messiah; John a prophet in both senses.
Ambrose: in prophesying of the Lord he rightly addresses the prophet; Origen: he hastened to speak, knowing John would soon dwell in the wilderness.
Calvin: he speaks of the infant's calling as already known, turning his eyes to God's purpose.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on Jeroboam's withered hand, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: Theophylact: Jesus is salvation, the knowledge of salvation given in John who witnessed of Christ; Bede: 'forgiveness of sins' lest a temporal salvation be thought.
The object of the Baptist's mission — to teach that salvation lies in forgiveness, not deliverance from outward enemies; the aim and end of the Forerunner's work.
Calvin: salvation consists in the remission of sins, God not imputing our trespasses (2 Corinthians 5:19).
Theophylact: forgiveness through mercy, not works; Chrysostom: the dayspring (Christ) has visited us, taking our flesh; the Greek Expositor: abiding on high yet present on earth.
'Tender mercy' literally 'bowels of mercy' (God's heart); 'dayspring' (anatole) the rising sun from on high (Isaiah 60:19); Lightfoot weighs 'branch' (Jeremiah 23:5) but the light favours the dawn.
Calvin: the mercy that made the day dawn, from the very bowels of God's mercy.

debates.question: Does 'dayspring' (anatole) mean the rising dawn or the Branch?

sides.position: The rising dawn/sun, since the giving of light follows

schools.summary: Bede: Christ the Day-spring disclosing the true light; Chrysostom: the darkness is error and ungodliness; Basil: the Gentiles in thick darkness till the rising light.
Rooted in Isaiah 9:2 (cf. Matthew 4:16): benighted travellers waiting for morning, the rising Sun guiding their feet into 'the way of peace' (Psalm 107:10), peace meaning all blessedness.
Calvin: out of Christ there is no life-giving light, all covered by the darkness of death.

Bede: the preacher of repentance passes his first years in the deserts, to reclaim men boldly from the world; Theophylact: his spiritual gift grew with his body; Origen: the Spirit ever growing in him.

The imperfects 'was growing' and 'waxed strong'; he dwelt in the hill country and deserts in obscurity till his public ministry at about thirty (Luke 3:23); Lightfoot weighs whether he was a true hermit.
